topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the force of a physical blow. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drum solo in the sudden silence of my apartment. I stared at the screen, the words blurring for a moment before snapping into sharp, horrifying focus. The headline was mine—my nuanced analysis of urban decay—but the byline beneath it belonged to a complete stranger. My breath hitched, catching somewhere in my throat. I scrambled for the original submission documents, my fingers slick with sweat as I reread the metadata. It was impossible. I felt a cold wash spread from my fingertips up my arms, leaving me shaking uncontrollably. Every muscle in my body locked up; I couldn't move, couldn't even lift the mouse to close the window. A low, ragged sound escaped my chest, a desperate plea for air that wouldn't come.

[story 2]

I reread the publication notice three times, the fluorescent light of my kitchen buzzing with an aggressive, irritating frequency. The realization hit me like a physical impact, stealing the air from my lungs. The article, the culmination of months of grueling research and sleepless nights, was attributed to 'Alistair Vance.' Alistair Vance. Someone else’s name was attached to my intellectual property. My hands began to tremble so violently I had to grip the counter edge to steady myself. I pushed back from the table, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor, a jarring noise in the quiet room. My vision tunneled slightly; the edges of the room seemed to swim. I stood frozen, listening intently for any sound—a knock at the door, a car pulling up—anything to break the suffocating stillness of the moment.

[story 3]

When the notification finally appeared, I didn't move. I sat down heavily on the edge of my bed, the cheap mattress protesting beneath my weight. My gaze was fixed on the screen, refusing to acknowledge the reality flashing before my eyes. The sheer audacity of it made my stomach clench into a tight, painful knot. I slowly lifted a hand, touching my neck, feeling the rapid pulse beneath my skin throb insistently. A dry, rasping sound emerged from my throat as I tried to force coherent thought, but only a hollow echo answered. Panic began to bubble up, hot and corrosive, making my skin prickle. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of the room like a trapped animal, my movements jerky and uncontrolled, my eyes darting around the room as if expecting the identity thief to materialize from the shadows.
